analogy: Think of "Electromagnetic Induction" through the chapter's own opening images: Physics 154 6.1 INTRODUCTION Electricity and magnetism were considered separate and unrelated phenomena for a long time. In the early decades of the nineteenth century, experiments on electric current by Oersted, Ampere and a few others established the fact that electricity and magnetism are inter-related.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 6.1 INTRODUCTION, ELECTROMAGNETIC, INDUCTION, 6.2 THE EXPERIMENTS OF FARADAY AND. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Electromagnetic Induction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that physics 154 6.1 INTRODUCTION Electricity and magnetism were considered separate and unrelated phenomena for a long time.

The text explains that in the early decades of the nineteenth century, experiments on electric current by Oersted, Ampere and a few others established the fact that electricity and magnetism are inter-related.

The text explains that they found that moving electric charges produce magnetic fields.

The text explains that for example, an electric current deflects a magnetic compass needle placed in its vicinity.

The text explains that this naturally raises the questions like: Is the converse effect possible?

The text explains that can moving magnets produce electric currents?

The text explains that does the nature permit such a relation between electricity and magnetism?

The text explains that the experiments of Michael Faraday in England and Joseph Henry in USA, conducted around 1830, demonstrated conclusively that electric currents were induced in closed coils when subjected to changing magnetic fields.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will study the phenomena associated with changing magnetic fields and understand the underlying principles.

The text explains that the phenomenon in which electric current is generated by varying magnetic fields is appropriately called electromagnetic induction.

The text explains that when Faraday first made public his discovery that relative motion between a bar magnet and a wire loop produced a small current in the latter, he was asked, “What is the use of it?” His reply was: “What is the use of a new born baby?” The phenomenon of electromagnetic induction Chapter Six ELECTROMAGNETIC INDUCTION

The text explains that electromagnetic Induction 155 is not merely of theoretical or academic interest but also of practical utility.

The text explains that imagine a world where there is no electricity – no electric lights, no trains, no telephones and no personal computers.

The text explains that the pioneering experiments of Faraday and Henry have led directly to the development of modern day generators and transformers.

The text explains that today’s civilisation owes its progress to a great extent to the discovery of electromagnetic induction.

The text explains that 6.2 THE EXPERIMENTS OF FARADAY AND HENRY The discovery and understanding of electromagnetic induction are based on a long series of experiments carried out by Faraday and Henry.

The text explains that when the magnet is pulled away from the coil, the galvanometer shows deflection in the opposite direction, which indicates reversal of the current’s direction.

The text explains that josheph Henry [1797 – 1878] American experimental physicist, professor at Princeton University and first director of the Smithsonian Institution.

The text explains that when C2 is moved away, the galvanometer shows a deflection again, but this time in the opposite direction.

The text explains that when the key is released, a momentory deflection is observed again, but in the opposite direction.

The text explains that 6.3 MAGNETIC FLUX Faraday’s great insight lay in discovering a simple mathematical relation to explain the series of experiments he carried out on electromagnetic induction.

The text explains that magnetic flux is defined in the same way as electric flux is defined in Chapter 1.

The text explains that the notion of the area as a vector has been discussed earlier in Chapter 1.

The text explains that if the magnetic field has different magnitudes and directions at various parts of a surface as shown in Fig.

The text explains that experimental observations discussed in Section 6.2 can be explained using this concept.

The text explains that mathematically, the induced emf is given by d – d B t Φ ε = (6.3) The negative sign indicates the direction of e and hence the direction of current in a closed loop.

The text explains that a uniform magnetic field of 0.10 T is set up across the plane in the north-east direction.

The text explains that (6.3), the magnitude of the induced emf is given by ( ) – 0 B t t Φ Φ ε ∆ = = ∆ ∆ –3 10 = 1.0 mV 2 0.7 = × And the magnitude of the current is –3 10 V 2 mA 0.5 I R ε = = = Ω Note that the earth’s magnetic field also produces a flux through the loop.

The text explains that example 6.3 A circular coil of radius 10 cm, 500 turns and resistance 2 W is placed with its plane perpendicular to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field.

The text explains that horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field at the place is 3.0 × 10–5 T.

The text explains that hence current is induced in the coil in such a direction that it opposes the increase in flux.

The text explains that this is possible only if the current in the coil is in a counter-clockwise direction with respect to an observer situated on the side of the magnet.

The text explains that to counter this decrease in magnetic flux, the induced current in the coil flows in clockwise direction and its South- pole faces the receding North-pole of the bar magnet.

The text explains that the direction of the induced emf can be found using Lenz’s law.

The text explains that they provide an easier way to understand the direction of induced currents.

The text explains that note that the direction shown by and indicate the directions of the induced currents.

The text explains that suppose that the induced current was in the direction opposite to the one depicted in Fig.

The text explains that a gentle push on the magnet will initiate the process and its velocity and kinetic energy will continuously increase without expending any energy.

The text explains that electromagnetic Induction 161 EXAMPLE 6.4 Example 6.4 Figure 6.7 shows planar loops of different shapes moving out of or into a region of a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader.

The text explains that determine the direction of induced current in each loop using Lenz’s law.

The text explains that it is placed in a uniform magnetic field B which is perpendicular to the plane of this system.

The text explains that it is also possible to explain the motional emf expression in Eq.

The text explains that the Lorentz force on this charge is qvB in magnitude, and its direction is towards Q.

The text explains that all charges experience the same force, in magnitude and direction, irrespective of their position in the rod PQ.

The text explains that therefore, to explain the existence of induced emf or induced current, we must assume that a time-varying magnetic field generates an electric field.

The text explains that in Chapter 4, we learnt that charges in motion (current) can exert force/torque on a stationary magnet.

The text explains that (6.5), the magnitude of the emf generated across a length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by d d Bv r ε = .

The text explains that electromagnetic Induction 165 EXAMPLE 6.7 Example 6.7 A wheel with 10 metallic spokes each 0.5 m long is rotated with a speed of 120 rev/min in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field HE at a place.

The text explains that the other end of the rod is in contact with a circular metallic ring.

The text explains that a constant and uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T parallel to the axis exists everywhere.

The text explains that calculate the emf developed between the centre and the ring.

The text explains that 6.6 A horizontal straight wire 10 m long extending from east to west is falling with a speed of 5.0 m s–1, at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field, 0.30 ´ 10–4 Wb m–2.

Do not add outside examples to Electromagnetic Induction; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 6.1 INTRODUCTION and ELECTROMAGNETIC to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
